questions.prompt: Complete: "I have ___ money in my pocket."
questions.explanation: In an affirmative sentence we use some: I have some money. Any belongs to negatives and questions, many goes with countable plurals (not money), and a few is also for countable nouns.

questions.prompt: Complete: "How ___ books are there on the shelf?"
questions.explanation: Books is countable and plural, so we ask How many books…?. How much is for uncountable nouns (How much water?), and "how a lot" / "how few" are not correct question forms.
After a negative (isn't) we use any: There isn't any bread. Some is for affirmatives, and many / a few go with countable nouns, while bread is uncountable.

questions.prompt: Complete: "We need ___ milk for the recipe."
questions.explanation: Milk is uncountable, so a small amount is a little milk. A few and many are for countable nouns, and "a lot" needs of before a noun (a lot of milk).

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the uncountable noun correctly?

options.text: She gave me a lot of information.

questions.explanation: Information is uncountable: it has no plural and works with a lot of — a lot of information. "informations", "advices" and "breads" are all wrong because these nouns are uncountable and never take an -s.
